title: A mathematical framework for dynamic emergent constraints in climate science
abstract: Emergent constraints in climate science are empirical relations that link the response to a forcing of a physical observable to the properties of other observables, with the aim of reducing climate change projection uncertainties. Here we use recent results in linear response theory to develop a mathematical framework for dynamic emergent constraints, a class of emergent constraints linking the response of different observables to the same forcing. We show how traditional dynamic emergent constraints are a special case of more general relations, that we call integral dynamic emergent constraints. These relations allow to compute the response of a predictand as the convolution of the response of a predictor and the proxy Green's function of the predictand-predictor pair. The conditions for the existence of integral emergent constraints are related to the causality of the proxy Green's function and the time scales at which the system is observed. We apply this framework to global warming simulations with the MPI-ESM climate model, to study dynamic emergent constraints between different observables. These results allow to put the theory of dynamic emergent constraints on firm mathematical ground, and suggest a protocol to identify necessary conditions for the existence of such relations in climate data.
summary: This work establishes a mathematical framework for dynamic emergent constraints in climate science by generalizing linear response theory. It uses proxy Green's functions and causality indices to determine how relationships between observables (like temperature and precipitation) are constrained by the observation time scale. This provides a powerful, mathematically grounded protocol for analyzing complex, non-equilibrium physical systems.
main_problem: To develop a rigorous mathematical framework for quantifying 'dynamic emergent constraints' between different physical observables in complex systems like the climate, aiming to reduce projection uncertainties.
main_result: The framework generalizes traditional constraints to 'integral dynamic emergent constraints,' showing that the existence and form of these constraints depend critically on the causality of the system's proxy Green's function and the coarse-graining time scale.
method: The study employs linear response theory, utilizing susceptibility ratios and proxy Green's functions derived from climate model simulations to establish integral relationships between observable responses to external forcing.
model_or_system: The analysis is applied to global warming simulations using the MPI-ESM climate model, focusing on observables such as global surface temperature, precipitation, and AMOC intensity.
key_observables: Global surface temperature (T_s), Precipitation (P), Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (M), and their respective responses ($\delta\Phi$).
important_parameters: Coarse-graining time scale ($	au$), which dictates whether the relationship is instantaneous, directional, or reciprocal.
main_assumptions: The theory relies on the assumption that the system dynamics can be described by a stochastic dynamical system amenable to linear response theory, and that the relationship can be captured by the proxy Green's function.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the causality index ($C_{\Phi_1\Phi_2}$) as a function of the time scale $	au$, showing how the predictive power and directionality of constraints change with the observation window.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the problem using linear response theory to generalizing constraints into integral forms. It then applies this theory to climate model data, analyzing how the causality index and proxy Green's functions reveal the dependence of emergent constraints on the coarse-graining time scale $	au$.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical machinery developed—using linear response theory, Green's functions, and causality indices—is highly transferable to analyzing non-equilibrium dynamics in open quantum systems, such as those involving dissipation or environmental coupling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
